common_name: Variegated Solomon's Seal (White-Edged Arching Shade Garden Perennial)

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Variegated Solomon's Seal spreads slowly by rhizomes — plant 18 inches apart and allow 2–3 seasons for plants to establish clumps and begin spreading to fill the planting area.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Variegated Solomon's Seal is one of the most refined and elegant shade garden perennials — producing gracefully arching stems reaching 18–24 inches, lined with alternate, oval leaves strikingly edged in crisp cream-white to ivory variegation that creates a luminous, layered effect in woodland shade positions. The arching, fountain-like habit of individual stems and the horizontal leaf arrangement create a distinctive two-dimensional, layered look unlike any other commonly grown shade perennial — the arching stems with their alternating variegated leaves fill horizontal space in a very natural, woodland-understory character. In late spring, small (1/2-inch), tubular, white bell-shaped flowers dangle from the leaf axils on the undersides of the stems — delicate, fragrant, and easily overlooked until examined closely. The foliage remains attractive through summer and begins to turn golden-yellow in fall before the stems die back completely for winter dormancy. In zone 9, Solomon's Seal is at the warm edge of its range — it performs best in full shade with consistent moisture and benefits from heavy organic mulch that keeps the root zone cool during Gulf South summers. The variegated form is significantly more ornamental than the plain green species, the cream leaf margins brightening dark shade positions where few other plants provide this degree of foliar elegance.

common_name: Beauregard Sweet Potato (Louisiana-Developed Leading Commercial Orange Sweet Potato)

planting.note: Plant certified slips after the last frost when soil has warmed above 65°F in well-drained, sandy loam to loamy, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5) soil in full sun; Beauregard produces best in loose, deep soil — heavy clay limits tuber expansion and produces misshapen roots; raised beds with amended soil are ideal in clay-heavy Gulf South soils.

directions: Plant certified sweet potato slips (rooted cuttings) after the last frost in well-drained, loamy to sandy loam, slightly acidic soil in full sun; Beauregard is the dominant commercial sweet potato variety in the United States — developed by Louisiana State University AgCenter and released in 1987 to address both production efficiency and consumer preference for the deep-orange-fleshed sweet potato that now defines the American market standard. The deep orange-copper skin over vivid orange flesh, consistently uniform tuber shape, and high sugar content that develops fully after curing represent the qualities LSU breeders targeted: a variety that performs reliably in the humid Gulf South climate while producing the attractive, sweet, deeply colored roots that American consumers prefer. Beauregard matures in 90–110 days from slip transplanting, making it one of the faster-maturing sweet potato varieties and allowing good production within the Gulf South warm season. Sweet potatoes require curing to develop their characteristic sweetness — newly harvested tubers are starchy and less sweet than properly cured ones. Cure at 85–90°F with high humidity (85–90%) for 5–7 days to heal skin wounds and convert starches to sugars. Plant certified virus-indexed slips rather than home-grown slips from grocery store sweet potatoes to avoid introducing sweet potato virus diseases that reduce yield. As a Gulf South native crop commercially centered in Louisiana, Beauregard performs with particular reliability in zone 8-10 humid conditions.

common_name: Burgundy Okra (Ornamental AAS-Winning Deep-Red Pod and Stem Okra)

planting.note: Direct sow 1 inch deep after the last frost when soil is warm (70°F+) in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; soak seeds overnight before planting to improve germination; harvest Burgundy pods at 2–3 inches for best tenderness — they toughen rapidly after 3–4 inches.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep after the last frost in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Burgundy is an All-America Selections award-winning okra cultivar with outstanding dual ornamental and culinary value — producing deep burgundy-red to crimson-red pods, stems, leaf veins, and flowers that make it one of the most visually spectacular edible garden plants available. The crimson coloration extends through virtually every part of the plant: the pods are deep red-burgundy rather than the standard green, the main stems carry the same red coloring, leaf veins are crimson, and the large (3–4 inch), yellow hibiscus-like flowers have a red center. The dramatic color creates an outstanding ornamental display in the summer garden that standard okra varieties cannot approach. The pods are edible and flavorful when harvested young (2–3 inches) — tender and mild, with the typical okra character. A critical practical note: the dramatic burgundy pod color disappears when cooked — the pods turn green when heated, which surprises first-time Burgundy okra growers. For the best ornamental and culinary effect, harvest pods at 2–3 inches (they toughen very quickly after 3–4 inches on this variety). In zone 9, Burgundy okra thrives through the brutal Gulf South summer when most other vegetables struggle, and produces prolifically from May through October with regular harvest.

common_name: Major Wheeler Coral Honeysuckle (Best Native Hummingbird Honeysuckle Vine)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Major Wheeler blooms most prolifically in full sun but tolerates more shade than most flowering vines — half-day sun produces good bloom in Gulf South conditions.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Major Wheeler is widely considered the finest cultivar of native coral honeysuckle (Lonicera sempervirens) — a vigorous, non-invasive, native vine producing intense scarlet-red tubular flowers in large clusters throughout the spring and early summer with rebloom through fall. Unlike the invasive Japanese honeysuckle (Lonicera japonica), Coral honeysuckle is well-behaved, manageable, and ecologically beneficial — a twining vine that climbs to 6–12 feet without smothering everything in its path, and one of the premier hummingbird plants in eastern North American gardens. 'Major Wheeler' was selected for exceptionally heavy and prolonged bloom compared to the straight species — the flower clusters are larger, more numerous, and the rebloom period more consistent, making it the overwhelming choice among coral honeysuckle cultivars for maximum ornamental and hummingbird value. The vivid red tubular flowers have no fragrance (unlike Japanese honeysuckle) but attract ruby-throated hummingbirds with exceptional consistency from spring through fall. The semi-evergreen, blue-green, oval leaves are attractive even when not in bloom. In zone 9, Major Wheeler performs best with afternoon shade protection during the hottest summers. Red berries persist into fall, providing late-season food for thrushes, mockingbirds, and other frugivorous birds.

common_name: Orange Clock Vine (Brilliant Pure-Orange Continuous-Blooming Tropical Vine)

planting.note: Plant container plants or transplants after the last frost in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Orange Clock Vine is a vigorous, fast-growing vine — provide a trellis, fence, or support structure at planting; the vine covers a 6–8 foot fence section in a single warm season.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Orange Clock Vine (Thunbergia gregorii) is an African climbing vine producing one of the most brilliantly pure-orange flower displays available in a commonly grown ornamental vine — the 1.5-inch, five-petaled, open-faced flowers are a vivid, saturated orange-to-apricot-orange with no dark center (the 'clock' of the common name refers to the open flower face), blooming in continuous succession from summer through frost. The pure, clean orange color without dark center markings or contrasting color zones creates a uniformly vivid orange effect that is quite different from Black-eyed Susan Vine (T. alata), which has a dark eye, or clock vines with throats of different colors. The fast-growing, twining stems cover trellises, fences, and arbors quickly — an established plant can produce a solid curtain of orange flowers across a 6–8 foot fence section. In zone 9, Orange Clock Vine grows as a warm-season perennial vine that may be killed by hard freezes but returns from the roots in protected positions with mulching; in zone 10-12, it grows as an evergreen perennial vine that blooms most of the year. The pure orange color makes it outstanding in tropical designs combining orange, yellow, and red — particularly effective with yellow allamanda, red mandevilla, and golden angel's trumpet for a warm tropical palette.
